items.question: In a typical CI pipeline, what is an artifact?
items.correctAnswer: An output produced by a build step, such as a binary or package
incorrectAnswers: A human approval email stored in git
A failed unit test log exclusively
items.explanation: Artifacts are the build products later stages can test, scan, or deploy.

items.question: What does CD often refer to besides Continuous Delivery?
items.correctAnswer: Continuous Deployment
items.explanation: Teams use CD for delivery (always releasable) or deployment (automatic production releases), depending on risk policy.

items.question: What does idempotent deployment scripts aim to mean?
items.correctAnswer: Running them twice leaves the system in the same desired state
incorrectAnswers: Each run must delete all servers first
They may only run on developer laptops
items.explanation: Idempotent automation is safer to retry and easier to reconcile with drift.

items.question: What is a common purpose of a deployment gate in CD?
items.correctAnswer: Require checks or approvals before promoting to the next environment
incorrectAnswers: Replace monitoring with manual logs only
items.explanation: Gates add policy between stages while keeping automation for promotion.

items.question: What is the main goal of Continuous Integration (CI)?
items.correctAnswer: Integrate changes frequently and verify them with automated checks
incorrectAnswers: Deploy once a year with a manual checklist only
Replace version control with shared folders
items.explanation: CI emphasizes frequent merges and automated builds/tests so issues surface early.

items.question: Why do pipelines commonly run tests before building container images?
items.correctAnswer: Fail fast on cheap checks before heavier packaging work
incorrectAnswers: Because tests require a finished image to exist first
Because Docker forbids tests in CI
items.explanation: Ordering stages from quick feedback to slower steps saves time and compute.

items.question: How do containers on the same Linux host usually relate to the host kernel?
items.correctAnswer: They share the host kernel and isolate processes with namespaces and cgroups
incorrectAnswers: Each container boots its own separate kernel
Containers cannot run on Linux hosts
They require a hypervisor for every process
items.explanation: Containers are not full VMs; they rely on OS-level isolation features.

items.question: What does `docker build` use as build context?
items.correctAnswer: Files sent to the daemon from the specified path or URL
incorrectAnswers: Only files already inside running containers
Only layers cached on the internet
Only the Dockerfile with no other files
items.explanation: The context is the set of files available to COPY and ADD during build.

items.question: What does the Dockerfile `FROM` instruction set?
items.correctAnswer: The starting image for the build
incorrectAnswers: The TCP port exposed at runtime only
The CPU limit for the container
items.explanation: `FROM` chooses the base layer; later instructions add more layers.

items.question: What is a Docker image composed of?
items.correctAnswer: Read-only layers stacked on top of each other
incorrectAnswers: A full guest operating system kernel per container
A single mutable file that grows forever
items.explanation: Images are layer stacks; containers add a thin writable layer on top.

items.question: What is a Docker volume typically used for?
items.correctAnswer: Persisting data outside the container writable layer
incorrectAnswers: Replacing the need for a network
Compiling source without a base image
items.explanation: Volumes keep data durable and can be mounted into containers at runtime.

items.question: What is the OCI Image Specification mainly about?
items.correctAnswer: A standard format for container images so tools can interoperate
incorrectAnswers: A cloud billing API for Kubernetes
A language for writing systemd units
items.explanation: OCI images let runtimes and registries share a common image model beyond one vendor.

items.question: In a typical workflow, what does `git pull` combine?
items.correctAnswer: A fetch plus merging into the current branch
items.explanation: `git pull` downloads updates and then integrates them, commonly by merge unless configured otherwise.

items.question: What does Git store for each commit besides the snapshot of files?
items.correctAnswer: A pointer to its parent commit(s)
incorrectAnswers: A copy of the remote URL
The diff from the empty tree only
items.explanation: Each commit links to one or more parents, which is how Git builds history as a graph.

items.question: What does `git rebase` do to commits compared with merge for integrating work?
items.correctAnswer: Replays commits on top of another base to linearize history
incorrectAnswers: Deletes all commits on the branch permanently
Creates a second copy of every blob in the repo
Only updates remote-tracking refs without changing commits
items.explanation: Rebase moves or reapplies commits so the branch history reads as if it started from a newer point.

items.question: What does a merge conflict mean in Git?
items.correctAnswer: Git cannot auto-merge overlapping edits and needs a human fix
incorrectAnswers: The remote rejected your SSH key
items.explanation: Conflicts happen when the same region of a file changed differently on two histories.

items.question: What is a lightweight tag in Git?
items.correctAnswer: A name that points directly to a commit object
incorrectAnswers: A tag that stores a full extra commit message object always
A branch that tracks a remote by default
A signed certificate for HTTPS remotes
items.explanation: Lightweight tags are simple refs to commits, unlike annotated tags which are separate objects with metadata.

items.question: What is the purpose of the staging area (index) in Git?
items.correctAnswer: To choose which changes go into the next commit
incorrectAnswers: To run automated tests before push
To encrypt secrets in the repository
items.explanation: You stage changes with add so the next commit records exactly what you selected.

items.question: What does a Deployment controller primarily manage?
items.correctAnswer: ReplicaSets and rolling updates of Pod templates
incorrectAnswers: Only TLS certificates for the cluster
DNS records for external users only
items.explanation: Deployments declare desired Pod state and roll forward or back safely.

items.question: What does a liveness probe help Kubernetes decide?
items.correctAnswer: Whether to restart a container that is stuck or deadlocked
incorrectAnswers: Whether to scale the Deployment to zero
Whether to delete the entire node
Whether to upgrade the cluster version
items.explanation: Liveness probes detect when a process needs restarting even if ports are open.

items.question: What is a Namespace in Kubernetes mainly for?
items.correctAnswer: Scoping names and resources within a cluster
items.explanation: Namespaces help teams share a cluster with separation for objects and policies.

items.question: What is a Service used for in Kubernetes?
items.correctAnswer: Stable networking and load balancing to a set of Pods
incorrectAnswers: Storing container images in the cluster
items.explanation: Services provide a consistent IP/DNS target while Pod IPs change.

items.question: What is the smallest deployable unit Kubernetes schedules onto nodes?
items.correctAnswer: A Pod
items.explanation: A Pod wraps one or more containers that share network and storage namespaces on a node.

items.question: Which component assigns Pods to Nodes?
items.correctAnswer: kube-scheduler
items.explanation: The scheduler filters and scores feasible nodes, then binds the Pod.

items.question: In distributed tracing, what does a span represent?
items.correctAnswer: A single operation with timing inside a trace
items.explanation: Traces are trees of spans showing parent-child work across services.

items.question: What does OpenTelemetry aim to provide for observability?
items.correctAnswer: Vendor-neutral APIs and SDKs to emit telemetry
incorrectAnswers: A single cloud-only database for logs
items.explanation: OpenTelemetry standardizes how applications produce traces, metrics, and logs.

items.question: What is a Prometheus metric type that only goes up or resets to zero?
items.correctAnswer: A counter
items.explanation: Counters measure totals like requests served; gauges can go up and down.

items.question: What is high-cardinality data in metrics?
items.correctAnswer: Labels or dimensions with very many distinct values
items.explanation: High cardinality can explode time series cost and query load if misused.

items.question: What three signal types are commonly grouped as observability pillars alongside logs?
items.correctAnswer: Metrics and traces
items.explanation: Metrics summarize behavior over time; traces follow requests across services; logs capture discrete events.

items.question: Why use structured logging instead of only free-form strings?
items.correctAnswer: To make fields queryable and machine-parseable at scale
incorrectAnswers: To remove the need for timestamps
items.explanation: Key-value or JSON logs enable filtering and correlation in log systems.

items.question: What does an SLO primarily express?
items.correctAnswer: A target level of service reliability for users
incorrectAnswers: A legal contract penalty clause only
items.explanation: SLOs translate user expectations into measurable targets like availability or latency.

items.question: What is a postmortem after an incident mainly for?
items.correctAnswer: Learning and improving systems without blaming individuals
incorrectAnswers: Deleting all logs from the event
items.explanation: Blameless postmortems focus on fixes and prevention across the system.

items.question: What is an SLA compared with an SLO?
items.correctAnswer: An agreement with customers, often with consequences if missed
incorrectAnswers: Exactly the same as an SLO with no differences
A private note only developers read
items.explanation: SLOs guide engineering; SLAs are external promises and may include credits or penalties.

items.question: What is an error budget in SRE practice?
items.correctAnswer: The acceptable unreliability window implied by an SLO
incorrectAnswers: The maximum lines of code allowed
items.explanation: If reliability is 99.9%, the error budget is the remaining 0.1% downtime or bad events you can spend on change.

items.question: What is toil in the SRE sense?
items.correctAnswer: Manual, repetitive operational work that does not improve the service long term
incorrectAnswers: Any kind of programming in Python
items.explanation: SRE aims to eliminate toil through automation and better design.

items.question: Why pair monitoring with alerting in production systems?
items.correctAnswer: To notify humans when measured signals need action
items.explanation: Monitoring observes state; alerting turns threshold breaches into timely response.

items.question: What does Terraform state track?
items.correctAnswer: The mapping between configuration and real-world resources
incorrectAnswers: User passwords in plain text always
items.explanation: State lets Terraform know what exists so plans can compute create, update, and destroy actions.

items.question: What does `terraform plan` show before changes?
items.correctAnswer: The actions Terraform would take to match configuration
items.explanation: Plan previews creates, updates, and destroys so operators can review risk.

items.question: What is Terraform primarily used to manage?
items.correctAnswer: Infrastructure as declarative configuration
items.explanation: You describe desired resources; Terraform plans and applies changes to reach that state.

items.question: What is a Terraform provider?
items.correctAnswer: A plugin that implements resource types for a platform or API
items.explanation: Providers translate Terraform resources into cloud or SaaS API calls.

items.question: What is a module in Terraform?
items.correctAnswer: A reusable container for multiple resources with defined inputs and outputs
items.explanation: Modules help standardize patterns like a baseline network or database setup.

items.question: What language are `.tf` files usually written in?
items.correctAnswer: HCL (HashiCorp Configuration Language)
items.explanation: HCL is structured for humans and machines and describes resources and providers.
